@Jennifer Hickey @Walter Martinez @Robert Hobbs
CC: @Daniel Arwe @Justin Pope

Let's clarify how this piece of AC can be verified:
we record the editor (user) and the date time of the change

There are multiple ways the theme can be modified today: group variable value change, child variable value change, and image change. Any of those changes will cause the modifier and modified date fields on the corresponding record in the ThemeVersion table to be updated. For example, if you update a child variable value for theme X version 2, then the record in ThemeVersion corresponding to theme X version 2 will show as just modified. Additionally, if you want more granular information, you could go specifically to the "theme child" tables like so: go to ThemeableGroupVariableValue for a group variable value change, ThemeableVariableValue for a child variable value change, and ThemeImage for an image change. All 3 of those tables have modifier and modified date fields that can be inspected if desired.

Please reach out with any questions or concerns.

As mentioned in standup, I changed my mind about calling this theme image category "Landing Page". It will instead be called "Dashboard". This new name better reflects the fact that the category applies to both the sign-in page and all dashboards (homebuyer, sys-admin, sales counselor, and any others). The new name also makes more sense in the codebase as "Landing Page" terminology was much less prevalent while "Dashboard" terminology was much more prevalent.

My responses to the most recent questions:

  1. Sure, I will change the permission description.
  2. Regarding which roles in production should get this permission granted, this may be a Rob question, but here is my answer if it means anything. I would not worry about it much. Even if we didn't turn it to Allow for anyone, the site will still function as expected. And additionally, no one is immediately wanting to change only the dashboard background. And if they do, they can figure out how to grant a Super Admin the permission in the UI. Now if we want to go in and look for a role similar to a Super Admin in each stack and grant the permission, I would be okay with that too.

I am simply honoring that existing functionality. It will be impossible for QA to test any functionality specifically targeted to only apply in a different environment, and that would include myveodesignstudio.com wanting a single image to serve as the background there today. What you can do, however, is upload a single image to the dashboard theme image category. Remember that if any image is uploaded there, then it will override any stack default. So then you would expect to see a single non-rotating image in VDS, and that would be a very close replication of the default functionality presiding in myveodesignstudio.com.
  4. Responding to the last question about various locations and landing page vs sign in page: I have changed the name of this theme image category from "Landing Page" to "Dashboard". Read what I said above. Also, it would have been great to cover multiple locations in this one story, but it was absolutely everything I could do just to get this one location working perfectly. And in the pre-story meeting, I warned about this, and Rob said this location only was acceptable. This location is undoubtedly the hardest one to get working due to multiple factors such as:
    1. the user is not signed in yet, so any API calls have to be unauthenticated and therefore cannot reveal any information even considered remotely sensitive
    2. I had to scrap the existing hard-coded "bgCycler" CSS strategy for rotating the background images and develop a custom JavaScript strategy for rotating a variable number of images
    3. the sign-page is routed to even before the shell of the app has a chance to know what themes exist in the DB; this results in a race condition since the sign-in page wants a theme applied immediately (just like all other views expect) but the sign-in page is different in that it can't have the theme immediately; so I had to figure out how to make it wait just long enough for the theme to be set so it would not error out

@Jennifer Hickey @Victor Monterrey @Robert Hobbs

Here is a Q&A for my implementation of the story.

Q: Where are we putting the ability for a user to add an image?
A: There is a new menu category in VeoAdmin called "Themes". There is a new view under the "Themes" category called "Theme Configuration". All configuration of a theme will happen in this view. Additionally, it was not in the AC, but in pre-story meeting, Rob said he eventually would want a new permission to restrict this view and that I should develop it if it wasn't much trouble. I have developed it. The new permission is called "Can Configure Themes" and the description is "Grants the user access to the theme configuration page". If you have a role with this permission allowed, then you will be able to see the new menu category and view. Also, in case your question took an alternate meaning, another answer to your question could be that the images are stored in a DB table called "ThemeImage".

Q: Are there any constraints on the image size or file types?
A: Yes. Let me discuss file size for the images first. I checked the file sizes on the existing 5 background images we have. 4 of them are between .3MB and 1.0MB while 1 of them was 1.8MB. For this reason, I have set the max file size for theme images to be 2.0MB. It can be changed easily if desired, so just let me know. If the user attempts to upload an image greater than the max file size, they will receive a friendly error message as seen in the VeoAdmin-only demo video. This is exactly how other file uploads are handled elsewhere in VeoAdmin. Now onto file types... The file types are restricted to .jpg, .jpeg, and .png. Any other file type should not be accepted. However, I believe it will not be possible to test an error message in this case because the upload dialog literally filters out invalid file types automatically.

Q: What is the timing on the rotation of images? How many images are in rotation?
A: It depends on the stack. All non-prod environments will use www.veodesignstudio.com below.
www.veodesignstudio.com: 2 photos, 3s crossfade, 60s idle
www.ccdi.veodesignstudio.com: 2 photos, 3s crossfade, 60s idle
www.denver.veodesignstudio.com: 2 photos, 3s crossfade, 60s idle
www.myveodesignstudio.com: 1 photo (no animation because only 1 photo)

Q: Will the user be able to name the image they upload?
A: No.

Q: To change an image does the user overwrite the one that is upload or can they add another and make it active while deactivating the first?
A: There is no concept of active or inactive for theme images. If it is uploaded, it is active, and will appear in the location designated by the theme image category (which right now is only 1 which I am calling landing_page). So if you don't want an uploaded theme image to appear, you must delete it or overwrite it by clicking the "Update" button and uploading a new one in its place.

Final thoughts not prodded by questions:
There is a concept of order among theme image categories which support multiple images (including landing_page). The image order can be seen in the UI and is 1-indexed in the UI. Hopefully we will improve upon the styling of this view in the near future. Also, as Rob mentioned in a standup meeting, for theme image categories which support multiple images, there will be no limit on the number of images that can be uploaded to the category. Finally, in the event that no theme image is uploaded for a given category (including landing_page), then whatever image(s) exist there today will be used rather than showing an empty white background. So this means that if you upload 1 theme image to the landing_page category of default theme, and if there are no corresponding images for the wild theme, then when you go in under wild theme, you will see the original background image(s) that you see today (not the new one uploaded to default theme).

Due to the way the responses were saved before, versus how they will be saved going forward...
-  Users who looked at the previous lifestyle snapshot, but did not make any color or style selections will see the "new" snapshot (new, provided it differs from the currrent VDS Default) should they come back to the lifestyle snapshot a second time.
-  Users who have looked at the previous lifestyle snapshot and made either a color or style selection, upon returning, will still see the previous snapshot

....This is due to the fact that, going forward, an empty response is saved for color/style, so that the lifestyle snapshot the user encounters upon their first visit will not change upon subsequent visits.  This is a departure from the previous way that responses were saved, in that they were only saved if the user made a color/style selection.  Now they are saved simply upon entry into the color or style step in the snapshot.  This is so the structure and options of the snapshot are preserved for that user, even of the organization changes their Lifestyle Snapshot in use.  If that is changed, it only affects new users going forward.  The questionnaire, previously and currently (prior to us rolling it into the lifestyle snapshot editor UI) is saved as a separate record in the homebuyer_orientation table.  Records there have two orientation_type values....'snapshot' for questionnaire, and 'colorGame' for color/style responses.

18925.text:
Developer Notes to Self:

Implementation Steps:
  1.  Extract logic for creating thumbnails with annotations/stamps overlayed.
  2. Implement the use of the newly extracted logic to generate thumbnails in the floorplanGallery VM that include annotations.
  3. Change route parameters for image editor to allow for loading of all plan images, rather than the current loading of a single image:
    1. Currently takes 'id' (floorplan Id)
    2. Refactor to take profilePlanId (so ALL images can be retrieved and used to create thumbnails) and floorPlanId (so a specific image is selected for editing first, based on which image they navigated to the editor by)
  4. Use the extracted thumbnail creation logic above to generate navigation thumnails in floorplanEditor/image editor.
    1. If only 1 image, collapse thumbnails panel (not needed)
  5. Add logic to select the correct starting image (based on route parameter implemented above)
  6. Add logic to scroll the navigation thumbnail panel to the selected thumbnail, bringing it into view
  7. Add logic (if keeping element) to update image label element above annotation stamps grid
  8. Add logic, on selecting a thumbnail, to load up the selected image into the canvas for editing
    1. The foundation for this already exists....
    2. ....the only difference is, currently, it loads up a single image based on the id passed in.
    3. We want to change it to load an image dynamically, based on the selection the user makes
  9. Add logic, on selecting another image (with pending unsaved changes), to prompt the user to save changes or discard.
  10. Add logic to collapse the gallery when only 1 image exists.
  11. Add logic to add floorplans to the list of floorplans, when new images are uploaded.
  12. Add logic to, on save, update the thumbnail images in the floorplan editor.
  13. Add logic to load the background images first, then bring in the annotations, reducing wait time for the user OR add a loading indicator
TEST:
  1.  Can the user see annotations on the thumbnails in the floorplanGallery?
  2. Can the user click on a thumbnail, in the image gallery, and be navigated to the editor, with the correct thumbnail selected there, and the correct image loaded into the canvas for annotating?
  3. On changing the selected image, do the appropriate elements update accordingly?
    1. label above annotation stamps grid (if keeping)
    2. image loaded into the canvas for editing
  4. Do all editing controls, stamps, zoom, etc. operate as they did previously?
  5. Is the navigation thumbnail panel hidden when there's only 1 image to edit? (TBD in prestory meeting)
  6. When attempting to navigate to next image, or back to the floorplan gallery, is the user prompted re: unsaved changes?  Same dialog as before?
  7. When navigating back to the floorplanGallery, are the thumbnails loaded up correctly, with the newly-saved annotations/stamps?

Prestory Meeting Questions:

Do we still need this element?
(On second thought, we will likely still want it, since there will be cases where only 1 image is present, making the thumbnail navigation unnecessary)



I'm thinking the navigation thumbnails will be hidded when there is only 1 image present.  Sound OK?
@Daniel Arwe @Robert Hobbs @Jennifer Hickey @Walter Martinez  I like this idea. Anytime we can reuse existing design and functionality I'm all for it.

@Charlie Bradley III, If you have no objections, I think for consistency, I'd like to rip off the design from the upload portion of the Plan Image Editor.  This would involve placing the thumbnail gallery from the image on the left into the space outlined in the image on the right (but likely a little smaller/more narrow.  Sound good?

CC: @Robert Hobbs, @Jennifer Hickey, @Walter Martinez

Then deleted the extended pricing, repriced the build, and confirmed that the pricing returned to the original build price.
Excel file attached: [3 pricing scenarios.xlsx](https://dev.azure.com/BuildOnTechnologies/ddde192e-e31e-4e51-af4b-3b7b4221266a/_apis/wit/attachments/53b9b1d6-e51e-4ad5-bde9-845b1159cfd8?fileName=3%20pricing%20scenarios.xlsx&download=true)

@<98EBA60A-973F-4D8F-90D4-24A629DD533B> 
Making notes for context :
Branch: dev/31790/flat_rate_pricing_implementation
- implementation notes are contained in a file in the repository [31790-flat-rate-pricing](https://github.com/buildontechnologies/VeoDesignStudio/blob/master/.copilot/plans/31790-flat-rate-pricing.md)
- make note that this is writen in a developer/LLM venacular and not geared to a business user

---

 Addressing Implementation Notes & Questions from below:

  override_builder_flat_rate / bypass_flat_fee_exclusion — these are the same concept. override_builder_flat_rate in the
  implementation notes was a naming misinterpretation of bypass_flat_fee_exclusion, which is the actual Echelon flag surfaced
  through the repricing pipeline. No separate flag is needed.

  IsFlatRatePrice on PriceDomainModel — not added. This was a misinterpretation of Echelon business logic. The flat rate
  signal is price_type = "area" on the field BOM line. IsFlatRatePrice was never a valid concept in this context.

  flat_rate price type — this string can come from Echelon but has no associated pricing model in VDS. osp_selItemPrice
  should never return it. No special handling is required; VDS ignores it.

  flat_rate branch in PriceBomLine options loop — not needed. The zeroing guard is applied inside 
  GetExtendedBuilderContribution / GetExtendedHomebuyerContribution directly, driven by bypass_flat_fee_exclusion. No new
  branch in the options loop was warranted.

  AdjustBomLinePriceResultBillQtyIfPriceTypeIsArea — commented out (not deleted). Preserves original build qty so repricing
  uses correct quantities without requiring a full BOM refresh if price type changes.

  Floor guard (AdjustBomLinePricing) — confirmed unchanged. A price change large enough to trigger the guard would arrive via
  a new price set, not the current session.

## 1 — Implement Flat Rate Pricing
**Description**  
Work with Jim and Echelon to confirm what we need to do to flat rate bom lines within builds. Then with this information modify existing backend pricing code to match what is expected.

**Implementation Notes**
- Evaluate `vds_vs_selItemPrice` and confirm the data model `PriceDomainModel` contains the information we need; make changes to support the Flat Rate Pricing Model
  - There is a new `override_builder_flat_rate` flag that we may need to account for — confirm with Jim what this flag controls (does it allow the builder to override the flat rate and enter a manual price? Does it affect the floor guard in `PriceBuild.AdjustBomLinePricing`?)
  - Confirm if `price_type` alone (e.g. `"flat_rate"`) will be enough to drive flat rate pricing, or if `override_builder_flat_rate` needs to be surfaced in `PriceDomainModel` as a new property alongside the existing `IsAreaPrice` pattern
- If `PriceDomainModel` needs changes: add `IsFlatRatePrice` computed bool property and surface `override_builder_flat_rate` if needed
- Implement the Flat Rate Pricing branch within `PriceBomLine`:
  - The options loop in `PriceBomLine.Invoke` currently handles `"percent"`, `"unit"`, and `""` (field line) — add a `flat_rate` branch
  - Confirm with Jim: for flat rate option lines, is qty = 1 always (fixed total), or is it multiplied by qty from `_buildRepo.GetOptionQtyForItemAsync`?
  - Confirm: is `AdjustBomLinePriceResultBillQtyIfPriceTypeIsArea` (which sets `BillQty = 1` for area items) relevant for flat rate — i.e., does flat rate also require a BillQty adjustment on the non-options path?
- Confirm `PriceBuild` needs any changes — likely `PriceBuild` is unaffected since it orchestrates `PriceBomLine` but does not inspect price type directly, though `AdjustBomLinePricing` (floor guard: never let repriced bom go below original price level price) should be reviewed for flat rate compatibility

**Questions to Confirm with Jim / Echelon**
- What is the exact `price_type` string returned for flat rate? (`"flat_rate"`, `"flat"`, other?)
- Does flat rate apply to the bom line itself (non-options path), the options path, or both?
- What does `override_builder_flat_rate` control — is it a VDS concept or an Echelon concept?
- Should the price floor guard (`AdjustBomLinePricing` in `PriceBuild`) still apply to flat rate items?

@Walter Martinez @Jennifer Hickey @Robert Hobbs

I had a one-on-one help session with Walter, teaching him exactly how to update things, but here is a reduced, printed version of the teachings. Walter: there should be no new information for you here, but of course, you can use this to refer back to.

We have 2 new tables: ThemeVariable and ThemeVariableValue. You can inspect the schema for yourself.

The ThemeVariable table gets populated from StaticData, so in non-prod environments, you do not have to run anything, but in prod environments, you do. The records in here represent a CSS variable. They are only optionally tied to a theme. Most are not.

The ThemeVariableValue table only gets populated via script, so you will have to script in the records for every environment. I have a script for each theme attached to this story. The records represent a CSS value which is attached to a theme and a variable. Note that you do not have to run the scripts, and nothing will break if you don't. If DB Themes feature flag is ON, but no scripts have been run, the locally supported value will be used for a theme's variable, so it will work as it does today. So the scripts are really just for the future when we will need VeoAdmin to modify the data.

You are probably curious about how to modify a theme variable value. You can do so with the following SQL command:

INSERT INTO ThemeVariableValue VALUES (NEWID(), 'taylor_morrison', 'CA639BDC-3BBF-4D71-A463-1742B4A71B44', '#11bbbb', 'reidw', GETDATE(), 'reidw', GETDATE());
You may want to replace NEWID() with a separately generated GUID so that you can keep track of it and delete it later. Replace 'taylor_morrison' with the desired theme to update. Replace the 2nd GUID with the desired variable ID to update. You can replace the style with whatever you want, but I cannot speak for what would happen if you put in an invalid style - probably just get ignored, but we can deal with that case later. You can put in whatever you want for author, modifier, createDate, and modifiedDate. They don't matter but are required. They could be useful when we get theme configuration implemented in VeoAdmin.

You are probably wondering what variable ID to use. Navigate to the ThemeVariable table and there you can grab the ID associated with a CssName of your choice (I know what you're thinking, gross). Sorry, but it's what we gotta do for now. I will give you some recommended testing variables, possibly with screenshots in an upcoming story comment.

By the way, I discovered a problem with the existing Perry theme. It is referencing 2 variables that are TaylorMorrison-specific for some unknown reason. I did not have time (and possibly the creative knowledge) to fix those references. And such references might break if I tried to test in such a way to exploit that. So I am simply excluding Perry theme from the scripts for now. No harm will be done to Perry theme without a script. It just means that even if the DB Theme flag is ON, Perry theme essentially acts like it is OFF and uses local styles. We should fix those rogue references in Perry sometime.

Please reach out with any other questions, and I will answer them as soon as I can.

Other Ideas (future work)...

indicate variables that exist in the global list, but are undefined in the current theme

19468.text:
@Robert Hobbs 

It's awesome that the bug has been fixed. Thanks for testing guys.

Regarding some insight into what regressed here, yes, I can provide that. The bug was introduced in recent theming work we had done. There is a location in the shell of the app that checks feature flags in order to grant the user access to certain routes. An effort was made to reduce loading indicators by reusing a set of feature flag values that had just become necessary earlier on in the app's lifecycle. Because these feature flag values now exist earlier on, it made sense for us to reuse these rather than fetch more of them when they are already in memory. If done successfully, it would reduce loading indicators (and wait time). But a weird Durandal concept was at play which made the 'this' keyword change context, causing a variable to be undefined. I forced the 'this' context to not be overridden; therefore this location now correctly has access to the feature flag values correctly without showing an unnecessary loading indicator.
@Reid Wilson can you give us some insight into what regressed here? I will need some background for the release notes.

Add QA tasks
  • Test theme in full for web design best practices, usability, readibility, etc

List of code changes:
  • "Clear All" text in Add Ons tab of cabinet selection wizard in Design Selections
    • the location has been variablized; no new variable was created
    • testing: affirm that this text's color matches the "Clear All" text color from the Swaps tab
  • the divider line underneath the "Profile Plan" text in the DMH disclaimer
    • the location has been assigned a different variable since the one it had previously was stupid; no new variable was created
    • testing: affirm that the color of the divider line matches the color of the "Profile Plan" text directly above it
  • the switch and active view name in the upper right corner of color coordinator
    • the location has been variablized; no new variables were created
    • testing:
      • the toggle should take the color or linear gradient as defined in the variable "switch-bg"
      • the active view name should take the color defined in the variable "color-primary"
  • new variable: 'color-unwishlisted'
    • this variable controls the color of the heart icon on items which are not wishlisted; the default theme is given a value of "var(--color-icon-primary)" for this variable in order to ensure the color does not change in default theme; additionally, no other themes are given a value for this variable; that means they will draw from default theme; since the default theme's value is a cleverly picked variable, all themes will continue to look the same
    • testing:
      • whatever you decide as necessary in regards to testing wishlist icons in existing themes
      • change the value of the new variable in any theme; ensure the new value is reflected in your chosen theme
      • the attached script will need to be run in any environment for which testing is taking place; DEV has already been run

    • all text in the app uses this font if the "font-main" variable is set to 'montserrat'
    • testing: see comment in #20384 for how to test support for a new special font; the instructions will ask you to ask the developer how the font was imported; the answer is it was imported by the Google Fonts CDN
  • added support for 'proxima-nova' font
    • all text in the app uses this font if the "font-main" variable is set to 'proxima-nova'

29073.text:
@[VeoDesignStudio]\VeoDesignStudio Team
In an effort to communicate the issue and fix, we were using a repository that was set up that is bloating the call. This repository (TenantRepository.cs) has the Tenant entity mapped with Account, Organization, Community, Series, and Plan. For this call we just want a list of the plans. So, the fix here is to simplify the repositories used to be more effecient with our EF Repositories.

21828.text:
@[VeoDesignStudio]\VeoDesignStudio Team
Jam board to do Architecture
https://www.figma.com/board/YGlhSOY7qHErlCBDxH2tTK/21828---Implement-Palette-Vars-in-DB?node-id=0-1&t=mYjCok0dDTSbO7Y5-1

#21751 the gradient calculation was solidified to the following:
    Tints and Shades calculation:
       With starting color (ex: primary secondary tertiary neutral success warning error), convert to a HSL value and
       calculate the range of the starting lightness value to lightest and darkest value.
       Depending on the ranges:
         1) if the ranges are greater than 30%
            - calculate the light and dark values by stepping 10% from the starting hsl value
         2) if the ranges are less than or equal to 30%
            - take the smallest range and calculate the biggest integer value that can be used to step the light and dark values
            - calculate the light and dark values by stepping the integer value from the starting hsl value

        note: whe do not want our lightest and darkest values to be #ffffff and #000000 respectively

Some notes about implementation: 
Justin proposes: 
  • use existing themablevariablevalues table to store both palette variables and non-palette variables (css level variables) 
    • palette_slot_1: color1 
    • bg-main: color2 
  • also include the palette variables in themeablevariables 
If the groups have no values in the DB and palette variables are defined, then the current engine won't dynamically resolve all variable values within a group. 

Also proposed is the idea that we might add a variable type to the table to differentiate between palette variables and css level variables.
Note: we need to decide at this point if we must continue to support the use of ThemableGroupVariableValues as defaults for undefined ThemableVariables in legacy themes (i.e. Flintrock, TM, etc.)

T1/2 STORY: Update theme engine to apply palette colors & support variable overrides
QA that new themes is using palette values in the DB
also existing themes are using the value defined in the themablevariablevalue table as overrides
devs would provide a report of overridden values for specific themes
select * from themablevariablevalue where theme_id = XXXX
or postman / theme end point to get the key value pair list
create the DB structure if needed for palette variables etc.
Justin says that palette variables would be themablevariablevalues

✅QA Validation Completed! 

We were able to test this bug with Reid's help. He set a new stack default background image. And I used a theme (default) with no background image set. Therefore, the stack-level image was displayed.

@Eric Hickey @Jennifer Hickey @Walter Martinez @Robert Hobbs @Joe Ebeling

I am sensing a need to recap absolutely everything about this work item, so allow me to do that now.

The bug was identified during deployment by Rob and Reid on October 14th. However, they produced a workaround to suffice until the bug could be fixed at its source.

Allow me to explain the existing functionality. The stack default background image(s) are set according to the hostname of the URL. For example, it is veodesignstudio.com for the WBS stack and myveodesignstudio.com for the EPLAN stack. But the non-prod environments are not specifically checked for; they fall under a default case of a switch statement which effectively assigns them the same images as the WBS stack. Since this functionality is environment-specific, you cannot test the EPLAN stack's default background image from qa.veodesignstudio.com.

I wrote up this bug, found the reason for the bug, and fixed the bug the next day. I notified the team of all this. For various reasons, the work item was brushed off for over a month. One reason was because the existing, temporary workaround in production was not presenting any problems. Another reason was the undecided procedure of how to test this story or wait until production to test.

In the multiple times since Oct. 15th which I have brought this up, I never dictated how it should be tested. I informed the entire team of the situation, provided ideas and recommendations, elicited ideas from other people, and attempted to have the team reach a consensus. Any deviation from the prior statement can be fact-checked by any Slack post or the testimony of any person contributing to a live conversation. But a consensus was never reached until the time of this writing.

Ideas presented for the testing plan included:
  • simply don't attempt testing in non-prod environments; test in production on deployment night; this was probably how the feature was originally conceived a long time ago
  • have a QA person watch "over the shoulder" of a dev running the app locally; the dev will "hack" the variable which gets assigned the URL's hostname to instead set it to that of an alternate stack; then, as the QA person drives the testing process as the dev is the copilot, the QA person verifies that the stack default background image is correct
  • create more cases in the code to check for dev.veodesignstudio.com and qa.veodesignstudio.com and set their images to something else
  • create 2 different release candidates; one with the bug fix and the other without; if the first one fails, then roll back changes and deploy the second one

The first of the above ideas drew heavy criticism for understandable reasons. The second idea was hardly commented on, so maybe people didn't really like it. The third idea was popular, but no one was certain whether or not the environmental prefixes (dev, qa, etc.) counted in the hostname of the URL. If so, it would work. If not, it would not work. The fourth idea was roughly considered acceptable.

So the bug sat like this for a long time.

Then finally some progress started happening. The team moved the work item into an active sprint on Nov. 21st. Then on Nov. 26th, the team had a conclusive discussion on what the testing plan should be. With Reid now having some availability, it was requested that he find out for sure whether we could target non-prod environments via hostname. The result is that, yes, we can do that. So with the approval of the team (although not the entire team since some members were not reachable due to PTO) (members of the developers, QA, and scrum master did approve the plan, also keeping in mind the wishes of others not present), we are moving forward with this testing plan. The DEV and QA environments will now have their own default dashboard background images. They are both currently set to a particular image also used by the EPLAN stack. The PREVIEW and STAGING environments remain unchanged. It was deemed not necessary to change them. If any team member has a complaint about these choices, please comment here, and we can discuss.

Now to answer any outstanding questions that didn't necessarily get answered above.

Q: Why can this not be tested in production?
A: It can. I think your question may be proposed in response to something written in the repro steps. I believe that the intention of the repro steps instructing not to reproduce in prod was to deter someone from replicating the issue in prod during regular work hours. This makes sense since it is common nature for people like us to want to replicate issues in order to better understand them. But for obvious reasons, that would be bad for production, except on deployment night. So I modified that text to say it's okay on deployment night.

@<16695B85-2F2C-6860-B106-056F5D5396E1> @<4C46EFE8-26F9-6F48-B257-971123DDB984> @<6E668580-F8AB-64C3-A4C1-1DDE45503DA2> @<D2E79038-97CA-6D63-8A21-A92E9A844981> @<A2D50473-D80B-69A8-9E7F-3B61DC1E3825>
**Ok, hold on.**  I feel like there's a LOT of information being swept under the rug here.
- Why can this not be tested in production?
- Is it reproducable anywhere else?
- How did we get to the point that QA feels the need to dismiss testing the bug outright?

This feels like an enormous breakdown in communication.  Clarifications are needed (for me, here), such that I understand how we are going to get back to the point where the _team_ has a plan to proceed and the quality of the application is not in jeopardy or relying on trust.

@Eric Hickey @Walter Martinez @Robert Hobbs @Reid Wilson @Joe Ebeling
Documenting testing expectations for the team here. This bug has been tested locally by Reid. Per Reid, it can go straight to production with no further testing needed. QA will assume, without further discussion, that no QA testing is needed and this will be tested once live in prod. QA recommends a roll-back plan be documented here to mitigate the risk in production.

If the Sales Counselor switches to user impersonation, the logic for selecting the C > S > P, from the Profile Plans for the impersonated user, is as follows:
*   Find the first CONTRACTED plan....return if found
*   Find the first COMPLETED plan...return if found
*   Find the first INACTIVE plan...return if found
*   If none of the above found, return undefined
The modOptionPricing dashboard module then determines what to display based on what is returned.
*   hasActivePlan() => User impersonation control (label showing C > S > P for active plan and "Launch Option Pricing" button
*   !hasActivePlan() => Sales Counselor control (C > S > P dropdowns)
So, filtering for Sales Counselor Dashboard will involve...
*   Filtering Community dropdown (non-impersonation view) for only Communities assigned to the SC user.*   We can likely do this here, when communities that populate the dropdown are loaded:

*   Filtering which plans to retrieve, from impersonated user's Profile Plans, to only include C > S > P combinations where Community is in the Assigned Communities list for the SC user.

@Daniel Arwe @Robert Hobbs @Reid Wilson @Roger Wang My team has looked into this. We believe the cause is long-running session reports. While the Azure function is listening, if the report takes too long, Azure will essentially kill the connection. On rare occasions, Azure will not kill the connection and the connection will time out. This is very similar, but gives different error message.

To combat the long runtime of the session report, we will be setting our timeout to 120 seconds. We have code to replace the Azure function in DEV and ready to be tested.

@Robert Hobbs, @Reid Wilson  This is the dead-letter queue from Azure Service Bus.  This screenshot shows this happening fairly recently.  @Roger Wang, @Andrew Fry, are there any other sources of information that might give us more diagnostic information for tracking down the cause of this error?

C:\Users\daniela\source\repos\VeoDesignStudio\BuildOnTechnologies.VDS.SessionCompletionReport\Functions\HandleCompletedSessionReportGeneration.cs



The code in the catch block here seems to suggest the error is happening when the API request is made in GenerateSessionCompletionReport, in this file....

Hey Justin, thanks for looking into this. Ok a few things from your commentary: 

  1. we need a work item to refactor our error handling here. Given that customers will be depending on the output for their integration processes (either by processing the excel file or calling our integration API end point), I am not certain writing "error" is a good idea. One option is to not write anything in the output. As option codes are missing the customer would then inquire as to why. This may or may not be better than them calling us to ask why they say "error". Error is informative enough I suppose to know there is a problem with our service, but not enough to know what went wrong. How much effort to refactor the error logging to be reliable and write something useful? How do we access that ? Is this log on a specific machine? If so I would rather write to event_log. 
  2. We need to have a conversation with Echelon about breaking interface. Of course they will change the behind the scenes logic but changing the signature is a problem. Should we look into a health check call from the uptime robot to this service that uses the specified URL and sends some nominal payload? We need notice when the service is down or they break contract because it means our service to our customer is not impaired and has to be adjusted. 
  3. As an output from this story can you document the agreed upon URLs for the call and where this is changed in the app? 
Thanks!

@Jennifer Hickey @Walter Martinez @Robert Hobbs @Eric Hickey

Sure, I will explain the CSS change I made. A CSS property of line-height was specified for the text elements reading "Primary Products" and "Secondary Products". I set it to 1.2 as opposed to "normal" (the default value). A "normal" line-height can differ between fonts, and the 'proxima-nova' font on those 2 text elements was pushing the buttons downward, causing the buttons to overflow their container. With the fix, the container/dropdown should remain the same size and the font size within the buttons should be the same. What you may notice is that only in Historymaker theme, there will be a tad less "space" above and below the 2 text elements I mentioned than there used to be, but it will align with what you see in all other themes.

@Reid Wilson @Walter Martinez @Robert Hobbs @Eric Hickey
What was the hard-coded css change? This determines our test strategy.

For example, did you implement that the pane is responsive and grows longer to meet the needs of a larger font? Is it a fixed size, but the font size gets smaller? This info will direct our testing. imagine you are explaining your code to a somewhat technical friend. Something along the lines of "Since the font could make the boxes take up additional length on the view, I made the pane grow in size to accommodate the lowest row of boxes, plus a bit of additional padding maintaining the current size fonts on the view" or maybe "The font on the view will reduce in size so the boxes always stay within the bounds of the pane".

Knowing what was implemented other than just "hard coded css change" allows QA to plan a test strategy. We would test differently if the pane grows unlimited to account for font than we would if the font reduces in size to never go beyond the bounds of the current pane.

@Jennifer Hickey  @Walter Martinez

The issue was related to the font which Historymaker uses, "proxima-nova". A hard-coded CSS change was made in this area which fixes the issue. No other areas should be affected. The buttons no longer overflow for Historymaker theme, and all other themes should appear the same.

22278.text:
@Jennifer Hickey @Walter Martinez  For testing, FYI...

Currently the issue is that this text is tightly coupled to a variable that is referenced by another element that is NOT insignificant.



Assumption:  Upon deployment...
  • ...ALL orgs not using Default as their theme will have DB Themes flag turned ON.  
  • ....All orgs using Default as their theme will have DB Themes flag turned OFF, to utilize the new themeable skeleton and associated palette
Given these assumptions are true, the approach to fixing this issue is as follows....
  1. Create a new unique variable specificly referenced by ONLY this element's (#daysToAppointment .moreInfoText) color property.
  2. Add new record for new variable in ThemeableVariable DB table, so all themes have it available in the editor for setting a value to override, if needed.
  3. Update the "themeable skeleton" (_variables.scss) file to point this new variable's value to a palette color that makes sense in default theme, which has the following consequence...
    1. Static theme (themeable skeleton) in SASS file will have a value for this new variable
  4. Add a value for this new variable in the DB default theme, resulting in the following effects....
    1. Default theme in the DB will have a value defined for orgs. using default with DB themes flag ON
    2. Other themess in the DB will have a value defined for orgs. using a custom theme with DB themes flag ON
  5. Add a new record in ThemeableVariableValue DB table for this variable....
    1. ...in the Default theme; all non-stylecraft themes will get their value for this variable via the reference defined in the Default theme
    2. ...in the Stylecraft theme that differentiates it from the value defined in the Default DB theme.
Scripts for addition of new variable and values on Default and Stylecraft are linked in parent story #21679

@<6E668580-F8AB-64C3-A4C1-1DDE45503DA2> , @<DED1F3BE-FA1D-6B76-B3AE-F3AE291DCC5F> , @<D2E79038-97CA-6D63-8A21-A92E9A844981> , @<4C46EFE8-26F9-6F48-B257-971123DDB984> 

Based on my research below, assuming a client-centric (frontend) approach to filtering, here are the big-picture changes as I see them....

Backend Changes:
- Modify DTO to return additional info. on the communties for each user's list of profile plans
- Leave the parameters for the endpoint, concerning current page and page size, the same.  This will allow other callers of this endpoint to continue calling it without modification.

Frontend Changes:
- Modify frontend mirroring DTO to receive the additional community info. passed from the backend.
- Implement client-side pagination logic to no longer call for a new search results list every time the page is changed, and instead display a subset of results, based on current page and items per page.
- Implement filtering of user results, based on the currently-logged-in user's assigned communities, the state of the feature flag, etc.

@<6E668580-F8AB-64C3-A4C1-1DDE45503DA2> , @<DED1F3BE-FA1D-6B76-B3AE-F3AE291DCC5F> , @<D2E79038-97CA-6D63-8A21-A92E9A844981> , @<4C46EFE8-26F9-6F48-B257-971123DDB984> Bear with my long explanation here.  I've uncovered some things that will inform our decision-making moving forward with this story.

Upon doing some additional research leading up to this story, I uncovered some interesting wrinkles that give us a few more things to think about in how we want to approach filtering the list of users returned from the search endpoint....

_HomeBuyerController.cs  => GetHomebuyerSearchResults method._

First of all, the frontend call to the endpoint takes the same amount of time with a page size of 8 as it does with a page size of 0 (in other words, with no limit to the number of items returned per page).  However, I should note that the time taken to run the same search in the DEV environment, on the server, was negligible, so I think this is of minimal concern. I tested this with a query that included a fetch of 131 objects.  The reason the time is the same for both is two-fold:

- The DB calls to retrieve the appropriate objects, via a series of stored procedures, is somewhat inefficient and fetches quite a bit more data than what is sent back on the DTOs returned to the frontend.

- Regardless of page number and page size requested, the entire list is retrieved and only after retrieval is it shaved down to the requested number of DTOs, via the pageSize parameter.
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Because of how the backend is fetching and returning the eventual DTOs, there are some potential issues involved with filtering the results client-side, but due to the fact that the query takes the same amount of time regardless of page size, there may also be a workaround.  Allow me to explain....

First of all, a challenge that we'll need to address is that, currently, the backend does not return any information on each user in the search result's profile plans, and thus returns no community information either.  However, this should be fairly easy to overcome as we can simply add on to what the DTO is returning to provide adequate information for the frontend to filter by the current (impersonating) user's assigned communities.

Second, the reason filtering in the fronted will be difficult, especially if we want to maintain a uniform number of users per page, in the paginated view we are using to display the search results.  Let's say the backend returns 8 out of 16 users for page 1, but 4 or them are to be filtered out by assigned communities.  Then page 1, would only show 4 results, whereas page 2 might still show 8....a rather peculiar UX.  There are several ways to overcome this challenge, a backend solution and a frontend solution.

- The backend approach, due to the fact that a lot of this is legacy code that is quite old, involving older use cases and data access relying on stored procedures, could involve heavy refactoring to get the older legacy use cases and our newer EF-based uses cases to play nicely together, and thus could increase development time significantly.

- The frontend approach, which I believe is the more efficient path development-wise, and allows us to continue leveraging the logic already built there to filter on assigned communities, involves fetching back ALL search results, and letting the client-side code handle the logic of displaying the current "page" of results when the user interacts with the pagination controls.  Since the backend fetches all results anyway, and yet only sends back the 8 or so results to the client, having a separate backend call each time the current page is changed is both inefficient and, I should note, takes the same amount of time on each page change (since the backend does the same fetch of ALL results for each call).  Therefore, the backend paged implementation buys us NOTHING in terms of efficiency and speed.  This alternative approach would allow the frontend to filter the entire list of results and then display the appropriate page with the appropriate number of results per page, as it does now, but without the repeated backend calls each time the page is changed in the pagination control.  It appears that the frontend pagination widget is very implmentation-agnostic, allowing the ViewModel of the parent view to drive the functionality triggered on various button clicks, which we could leverage to implement a client-side-driven pagination approach.

I know this is a lot to take in, but hopefully this will help drive a discussion of how we want to move forward on this story.

@<6E668580-F8AB-64C3-A4C1-1DDE45503DA2> , I was poking around on this story and saw this widget on the SC dashboard.  Should this be handled in this story?  I'm assuming that, since we didn't include this piece in the scoring, that it should likely live in its own future story.  Please advise.

  1. Eventually we will need a way for them to adjust the days forward to pull in provisional appointments into this new tab. So, one idea I would propose is that for this story we have a visual text box or drop down that is read only that defaults to the number of days to fetch for provisional appointments and we load those in this dialog. Later we could make that editable somehow and refresh the tab with provisional appointments going out a different length of time. Given that we are quite close to sprint end and this story may carry you could also just handle the change in days now. 
  2. Sure, if we are using Reject then let's stick with that. 
  3. An additional dialog is not great. Maybe they can click Approve and the buttons switch to "Yes, Approve" and "Cancel" which reverts back to the Approve / Reject buttons?

@Robert Hobbs, There are a few key pieces of information I was wondering about before I get too deep into this story.
CC: @Walter Martinez, @Jennifer Hickey
Questions:
  1. I am assuming that, because the AC says to filter "going out to X days where the value is pulled from the user settings dialog for Load X days," and "the user would only see provisional appointments for buyers who fall into the current scheduler filter rules and the organizations / locations configured for that user", that the controls highlighted here would still only apply to (and be visible on) the current Confirm Appointments tab for non-provisional appointments.  In other words, there will not be a need for a date or a builder filter on the Provisional Appointments tab.  Is this correct?


  2. Is it OK to use "Reject" instead of "Deny" for the dropdown on what action to take on a given provisional appointment, since that is the current verbiage elsewhere in the UI?
  3. When the user selects "Approve", what mechanism do we want for confirmation that is the action they want to take?  On the schedule toolbar button click to approve, we confirm with a popup/dialog, but since we are already in a dialog here, and dialog over dialog is not ideal (although we do it in a few other places in scheduler), how do we want to confirm? Some options might be....
    1. Button on the row matching the button on the schedule (good for consistency).
      1. Single-click approval (no confimation); no way to undo approval
      2. Double-click approval
        1. First click changes button to OK/Cancel buttons
        2. Second click either confirms (OK) or cancels.
    2. Similar to above, but to save horizontal space, perhaps we have rows that expand to reveal confirmation actions
      1. For approval => simple OK / Cancel
      2. For reject/deny => textarea for reason; OK / Cancel buttons
    3. Dialog over dialog; while not ideal, we may already need to do this with the Buyer Info. Dialog, so not sure it's totally out of the question for confimation/additional info (reason) for approve/reject.

https://dev.azure.com/BuildOnTechnologies/VeoDesignStudio/_git/VeoDesignStudio/pullrequest/9015

@<6E668580-F8AB-64C3-A4C1-1DDE45503DA2> , @<A2D50473-D80B-69A8-9E7F-3B61DC1E3825> Added to current sprint at Rob's request....

![image.png](https://dev.azure.com/BuildOnTechnologies/ddde192e-e31e-4e51-af4b-3b7b4221266a/_apis/wit/attachments/9164eb52-bd9c-4038-8877-78a6893e3fc5?fileName=image.png) 
![image.png](https://dev.azure.com/BuildOnTechnologies/ddde192e-e31e-4e51-af4b-3b7b4221266a/_apis/wit/attachments/715d2526-001c-4c51-a02e-937a3dcab83e?fileName=image.png)

The root of the issue is that the OptionPricingGroup's constructor is not checking for a defined PriceLevelMasterList on the data coming in from the backend.  Since it comes in null in this case, the call to .map on the array causes the error.

![image.png](https://dev.azure.com/BuildOnTechnologies/ddde192e-e31e-4e51-af4b-3b7b4221266a/_apis/wit/attachments/4882be86-2d11-4423-8fca-b6ce4b7de76d?fileName=image.png) 

![image.png](https://dev.azure.com/BuildOnTechnologies/ddde192e-e31e-4e51-af4b-3b7b4221266a/_apis/wit/attachments/f55c042b-0e88-4578-97fb-88871fadee45?fileName=image.png) 

![image.png](https://dev.azure.com/BuildOnTechnologies/ddde192e-e31e-4e51-af4b-3b7b4221266a/_apis/wit/attachments/0106ed6e-1a92-4c0e-b906-a34712c5bbb2?fileName=image.png) 

The fix implemented was to check PriceLevelMasterList prior to calling map.  If it's not defined, priceLevelMaster list on the frontend model stays as an empty array.

![image.png](https://dev.azure.com/BuildOnTechnologies/ddde192e-e31e-4e51-af4b-3b7b4221266a/_apis/wit/attachments/73792970-b47a-4255-be09-c6d21997e013?fileName=image.png)

26411.text: Selecting elevation B for DMH

✅QA Validation Completed!

PFA some screenshots

For VCMS


Elevation Specific for DMH: Selecting Elevation B for testing

Documentation

When creating a new session, the current state of the builder's catalog will be used. Catalog items which are near duplicates except for elevation, option id, and price will not show up as duplicates. The elevation on the user's profile plan will determine which catalog item to pick; the elevations must match. There were no code changes to DMH or the import process from OP to DMH in this story; however, there are some notable implications. Since near duplicate catalog items can now be marked as option pricing display true without showing as duplicates in OP, a catalog item with the required elevation can now show up in DMH. Note that a price discrepancy between what the buyer saw in OP and DMH is possible. If there is one, the price in DMH will always be lower.

It is worth mentioning that VDS was actually already filtering out these near duplicate items for OP in the case of VCMS catalog source provider only. The change in this story only has an impact on the VDS catalog source provider.

Additional info from Jacob: 

The reason we include categories is because of examples like below. We only need the highest priced version for each tenet and category pair showing in option pricing.

Jacob sent this to me today, indicating the logic for what they decide to mark as visible: 


Here are the fields we use for assigning option pricing display when we have elevations.


Community, Series, Plan, Application, Product, and then item number or item description if there is no item number.
From there we sort by price and set the highest to TRUE.

Import to DMH (from option pricing) 
  • when importing selected options into DMH, the target session may not have the precise catalog item (by option code) because of the elevation but may have a package or catalog item matching the item number. We would want to select that.

28613.text:
Note about Design My Home using sub-optimal routing techniques:

We discovered a bug during this story which logged the user out and re-routed them to the sign-in page just after clicking "Accept" on the DMH disclaimer to enter DMH, but only when the logged in user logged in using the Microsoft 3rd party authentication provider. After some debugging, we discovered a query parameter was not being cleared when Microsoft redirects the user back to our site. Clearing that extra unnecessary query parameter fixed the issue.

The reason why that change fixes the issue is because DMH currently attempts to perform a full-page reload every time the user tries to navigate to a child route of DMH. Since Durandal is a SPA JS-framework, this is not recommended, as it does not have to do that. We should be leveraging Durandal's routing capabilities to handle child routing. The reason that it has been working all of this time is that the base URL does not change on the full-page reload; however, with that extra Microsoft query parameter in the base URL, the full-page reload had noticeable effect. We could have a tech debt story to improve the child routing within DMH, but it may not be worth it since the Angular migration is supposedly coming up soon.

19238.text:
@Jennifer Hickey @Joe Ebeling @Daniel Arwe @Reid Wilson @Justin Pope @Eric Hickey @Walter Martinez 

Jennifer Wrote: Currently, for Flintock, there are very few common variables within each group. For example, a group of 8 may have 2 unique and 6 different.

Can we speculate on how best to handle Flintrock under this structure? My understanding at this point is that we will be scripting the current values but the group level (palette) level variables are not so helpful, but the site will still function as-is. Does this mean we need to refactor their values into workable groups as a defined effort?

Putting my thoughts in words to discuss, if needed, after @Robert Hobbs has seen the UI/workflow.

Looking at the work for the simplified theme editor, I believe we have missed the mark meeting the intended business value. The editor itself is doing what was asked. There are groups and a value can be set for all children in that group, but using the groupings as defined on the trello board does not allow us to meet the business need outlined in the current story. It says "We have over 250 theme variables that have come out of just naming things in the UI with something unique. Many of them will share values or be assigned to others. We want to simplify the set of managed variables to a low a set as possible and still style the site." and the current groupings does not meet this need.

My understanding based on this part - Many of them will share values - was that a group is expected to, for the most part, have the same value so the user would set the group's value and all/most children would get that value, eliminating the need for the end user to set each value individually.

Currently, for Flintock, there are very few common variables within each group. For example, a group of 8 may have 2 unique and 6 different. Currently, this makes setting a the group variable have little value. The user will still have to go to most all child variables to set them individually. The current groupings don't allow us to meet the business goal you outlined. (I am basing this information on the current Default and Flintrock themes.)

The groupings does make the editor easier to read. Meaning, I can find the variable I need easier in the list

@Jennifer Hickey @Robert Hobbs @Reid Wilson @Walter Martinez @Justin Pope @Joe Ebeling @Charlie Bradley III @Eric Hickey 
Spot checking is fine with us devs and would provide an adequate level of assurance that all is behaving as expected.  We also don't want the testing effort to inflate the score on the story.  To that end, as developers, we are also testing as development continues, so we feel that the risk of breaking changes is relatively low.

Also, @Robert Hobbs, to reiterate what we discussed in standup, we have a script to migrate values from the old to the new data structures, so we should not encounter any issues with existing themes breaking.

@Robert Hobbs @Reid Wilson @Walter Martinez @Daniel Arwe @Justin Pope @Joe Ebeling @Charlie Bradley III @Eric Hickey 
Test Stragegy was discussed at grooming and in a conversation below and the team wanted limited spot checking between the UI and the trello board. The plan discussed at refinement was to select 5-10 css variables and ensure they are set correctly in the UI. We also planned to change a couple of group level and a couple of lower level variables and ensure the change appeared in the UI. As Reid mentioned below, the idea was that "Checking several relationships across a couple groups should be sufficient." There was no intention to check that each variable is the same for each theme in the DB after this work is complete. 

The developers will need to speak to the level of risk in a theme's variable's CSS changing unexpectedly during this work.

As for test strategy - 
Spot checking was the basis of QA's score of 8. It does not ensure every variable is correct for live themes. In fact, QA has not, up to this point, verified any theme is actually correct. QA has only ensured there is no green unexpectedly once a theme is applied.

If the team would like to amend the test strategy, QA is happy to accommodate, but the team should plan for the extended time needed. The most extensive testing could include scrutenizing each live theme. To do this, QA will need to know - the UI plan for each theme, a list of each variable and its expected value for each live theme and where those variables align to the UI. 

An intermediate level testing could include we spot checking as described above, plus visually checking each live theme for the areas those themes have been applied. This would not compare what we had vs what we have now, but check that nothing looks off.

The team will need to plan which test strategy is prefered based risk of issues arising and their knowledge of what is changing.
